seo.title: Healthcare Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for code generation in healthcare environments introduces compounding risks: a single misconfigured agent can simultaneously expose PHI, generate unsafe clinical logic, and violate HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and HITECH Act requirements in a single execution cycle. Unlike general software development, healthcare code generation agents operate in a domain where bugs are not just technical failures — they can directly cause patient harm, trigger malpractice liability, and result in regulatory fines up to $1.5M per violation. This checklist provides the concrete, auditable controls required to deploy LLM-based code generation agents safely and compliantly in healthcare settings.

sections.heading: PHI Protection & Data Boundary Controls
sections.description: Ensure that no Protected Health Information enters the LLM processing pipeline and that all data flows comply with HIPAA minimum necessary standards and HITECH breach notification requirements.

items.label: Implement PHI De-identification Gateway
items.description: Deploy an automated de-identification layer (per HIPAA Safe Harbor or Expert Determination method) that scrubs all input data before it reaches the LLM agent. Validate against 18 HIPAA identifiers including patient names, MRNs, dates of service, and IP addresses.

items.label: Enforce Data Residency Boundaries
items.description: Configure network egress controls to ensure patient data and generated code referencing PHI schemas never leave the approved processing environment. Use VPC-level isolation and block all outbound LLM API calls that carry raw clinical data.

items.label: Audit All LLM Input/Output Payloads
items.description: Log every prompt sent to and response received from the LLM agent with tamper-evident audit trails stored in a WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage system. Logs must include timestamps, user identity, and session IDs to satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail requirements.

items.label: Scan Generated Code for PHI Pattern Leakage
items.description: Run automated regex and NLP-based scanners on all LLM-generated code outputs to detect hardcoded PHI patterns (SSNs, DOBs, MRNs, patient names) before the code is committed to any repository or executed.

items.label: Classify and Tag All Input Data
items.description: Implement a data classification pipeline that tags all inputs as PHI, de-identified, or synthetic before routing to the agent. Reject any unclassified data at the agent boundary with an automatic alert to the compliance team.

items.label: Restrict LLM Context Window to Minimum Necessary Data
items.description: Apply HIPAA minimum necessary principle to agent context construction — only include schema definitions, anonymized sample data, and functional requirements in the LLM prompt. Never include real patient records as few-shot examples.

items.label: Implement Consent and Authorization Checks Before Code Execution
items.description: Verify that any code generated to process patient data includes explicit authorization scope checks aligned with patient consent records. Flag and block generated code that accesses data categories beyond the consented scope.

sections.heading: Code Safety & Clinical Logic Validation
sections.description: Healthcare code generation agents must produce code that is not only syntactically valid but clinically safe — errors in dosage calculations, diagnostic logic, or data transformations can directly harm patients.

items.label: Enforce Mandatory Static Analysis Before Execution
items.description: Run all LLM-generated code through a static analysis pipeline (e.g., Semgrep, Bandit for Python, SonarQube) configured with healthcare-specific rulesets that flag unsafe arithmetic operations, division-by-zero risks in dosage calculations, and unvalidated clinical inputs.

items.label: Require Human Clinical Review for Safety-Critical Code Paths
items.description: Any generated code that touches clinical decision logic (drug interactions, dosage calculations, diagnostic thresholds, alert triggers) must be reviewed and approved by a licensed clinician or clinical informaticist before deployment. Document this review in the audit trail.

items.label: Validate Syntax and Semantic Correctness via Automated Test Suite
items.description: Execute generated code against a pre-defined test suite that includes boundary condition tests, null input handling, and clinical edge cases (e.g., pediatric vs. adult dosing ranges). Require 100% test pass rate before code proceeds to staging.

items.label: Implement Unit Test Generation as Part of Agent Output
items.description: Configure the LLM agent to generate corresponding unit tests alongside every code artifact. Tests must cover happy paths, error conditions, and clinically relevant edge cases. Validate that generated tests are not trivially passing (e.g., assert True).

items.label: Check for Unsafe Type Coercions in Clinical Data Handling
items.description: Scan generated code for implicit type coercions that could corrupt clinical values — for example, integer truncation of decimal lab values, string-to-date parsing errors in medication schedules, or float precision loss in weight-based dosing.

items.label: Validate Against HL7 FHIR and Clinical Data Standards
items.description: For code that generates or consumes clinical data structures, automatically validate that generated schemas and data mappings conform to HL7 FHIR R4 or applicable standards (ICD-10, SNOMED CT, LOINC). Reject non-conformant outputs.

items.label: Require Iterative Refinement Loops to Include Safety Regression Tests
items.description: During the agent's iterative code refinement cycles, ensure each iteration re-runs the full safety test suite — not just the tests for the changed component. Prevent regression of previously passing clinical safety checks.

items.label: Document Clinical Assumptions in Generated Code Comments
items.description: Configure the agent prompt to require explicit inline documentation of all clinical assumptions (e.g., assumed unit of measure, assumed patient population, reference guideline version). Flag generated code lacking these annotations for human review.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Dependency Security
sections.description: The code execution sandbox must be hardened to prevent escape, lateral movement, and supply chain attacks — particularly critical when generated code may interact with EHR systems, clinical databases, or medical device APIs.

items.label: Execute All Generated Code in Isolated Container Sandboxes
items.description: Run generated code exclusively in ephemeral, network-isolated containers (e.g., gVisor, Firecracker microVMs) with no access to production EHR systems, patient databases, or external networks. Destroy containers after each execution cycle.

items.label: Scan All Generated Dependencies Against Known Vulnerability Databases
items.description: Before installing any package or library referenced in generated code, scan against NVD, OSV, and healthcare-specific vulnerability feeds using tools like Trivy or Snyk. Block installation of packages with CVSS score ≥ 7.0 without explicit security team approval.

items.label: Enforce Allowlist-Based Package Installation Policy
items.description: Maintain a curated allowlist of approved libraries for healthcare code generation (e.g., approved HL7 parsers, cryptography libraries). Reject any generated code that attempts to import packages outside the approved list, and alert the security team.

items.label: Detect and Block Credential Leakage in Generated Code
items.description: Run automated secret scanning (e.g., TruffleHog, GitLeaks) on all generated code outputs before execution or commit. Specifically scan for API keys, database connection strings, OAuth tokens, and hardcoded passwords referencing clinical systems.

items.label: Implement Resource Quotas and Execution Time Limits in Sandbox
items.description: Set strict CPU, memory, disk I/O, and execution time limits on sandbox containers to prevent denial-of-service conditions and detect potentially malicious generated code attempting resource exhaustion. Alert on any execution exceeding defined thresholds.

items.label: Monitor Sandbox Network Calls for Unauthorized Exfiltration Attempts
items.description: Deploy network monitoring within the sandbox environment to detect and block any outbound connection attempts made by generated code. Log all attempted connections for forensic review, as exfiltration attempts may indicate prompt injection or supply chain compromise.

items.label: Validate Dependency Integrity via Cryptographic Hash Verification
items.description: Verify the cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of all installed packages against the official registry manifest before execution. This prevents dependency confusion attacks where a malicious package could be substituted for a legitimate healthcare library.

items.label: Implement Sandbox Escape Detection and Automatic Incident Response
items.description: Deploy runtime security monitoring (e.g., Falco) to detect sandbox escape attempts, privilege escalation, or unexpected syscalls from generated code. Automatically terminate the session, quarantine artifacts, and trigger a security incident workflow.

sections.heading: Audit, Governance & Regulatory Compliance
sections.description: Healthcare AI code generation agents must maintain complete, tamper-evident audit trails and governance controls to satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records requirements, SOC 2 Type II audit obligations, and HIPAA accountability rules.

items.label: Maintain FDA 21 CFR Part 11-Compliant Electronic Audit Trails
items.description: Ensure all agent actions — prompt submissions, code generations, test executions, human approvals, and deployments — are recorded in a time-stamped, tamper-evident audit log with unique user identification. Logs must be retained for a minimum of 2 years and be exportable for FDA inspection.

items.label: Implement Role-Based Access Control for Agent Interaction
items.description: Restrict access to the code generation agent based on least-privilege RBAC policies. Define distinct roles for developers, clinical reviewers, compliance officers, and auditors. Require MFA for all agent interactions involving clinical system code generation.

items.label: Establish a Formal Change Control Process for Agent-Generated Code
items.description: Require all agent-generated code destined for production healthcare systems to pass through a documented change control process including risk assessment, clinical safety review, security review, and documented approval signatures — consistent with FDA software validation requirements.

items.label: Conduct Regular Bias and Safety Audits of Agent Outputs
items.description: Schedule quarterly audits of a statistically significant sample of agent-generated code to assess for systematic biases (e.g., assumptions about patient demographics), safety anti-patterns, and drift from clinical best practices. Document findings and remediation actions.

items.label: Define and Test Incident Response Procedures for Agent Failures
items.description: Document specific incident response playbooks for scenarios including: PHI exposure via generated code, deployment of clinically unsafe code, sandbox breach, and credential leakage. Conduct tabletop exercises at least annually and after any significant agent architecture change.

items.label: Map Agent Controls to SOC 2 Type II Trust Service Criteria
items.description: Document how each technical control in the agent deployment maps to SOC 2 Type II criteria (Security, Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity). Provide this mapping to your external auditor and update it with each major agent configuration change.

items.label: Implement Automated Compliance Reporting for HIPAA Risk Assessments
items.description: Configure the agent platform to generate automated reports on PHI exposure risk, de-identification failures, and access anomalies. These reports should feed directly into your organization's annual HIPAA Security Rule risk assessment documentation.

notes.note: Under HIPAA's Security Rule (45 CFR § 164.312), any AI agent that generates code processing ePHI is considered part of your technical safeguards infrastructure and must implement access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security. The HITECH Act increases penalties to $1.5M per violation category per year and mandates breach notification within 60 days — a single instance of PHI leaking through a code generation agent's output constitutes a reportable breach.

If the generated code is used in FDA-regulated activities (clinical trials, medical device software, laboratory systems), all electronic records and signatures associated with the agent's outputs must comply with 21 CFR Part 11. This means audit trails must be computer-generated, time-stamped, and protected from modification. The agent itself may be considered part of a validated computer system requiring Installation Qualification (IQ), Operational Qualification (OQ), and Performance Qualification (PQ) documentation.

notes.regulation: FDA Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)
notes.note: If the LLM code generation agent produces software that meets the FDA's definition of Software as a Medical Device — particularly code that influences clinical decision-making — it may require FDA premarket submission (510(k) or De Novo) and must follow the FDA's AI/ML-Based SaMD Action Plan, including predetermined change control plans (PCCPs) for any iterative model updates.

Healthcare organizations deploying AI code generation agents must ensure the agent platform and all integrated systems are included in the SOC 2 Type II audit scope. The Processing Integrity trust service criterion specifically requires that system processing is complete, valid, accurate, timely, and authorized — all of which must be demonstrably enforced through the agent's validation pipeline, sandbox controls, and human review gates documented in this checklist.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your healthcare AI code generation agents against HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and SOC 2 Type II controls — turning this checklist into automated, real-time compliance enforcement.
